By rsj, history, 3 years ago,

1787A - Exponential Equation

Idea & Solution: rsj

Tutorial

1787A - Exponential Equation

For even nn, a key observation is that x=1,y=n2x=1,y=n2 is always legit.

And for odd nn, notice that xyy+yxx=xy(xy−1+yx−1)xyy+yxx=xy(xy−1+yx−1). So if xx or yy is an even number, obviously xyy+yxxxyy+yxx is an even number. Otherwise if both xx and yy are all odd numbers , xy−1xy−1 and yx−1yx−1 are all odd numbers, so xy−1+yx−1xy−1+yx−1 is an even number, and xyy+yxxxyy+yxx is also an even number. That means xyy+yxxxyy+yxx is always even and there's no solution for odd nn.

1787B - Number Factorization

Idea & Solution: rsj

Tutorial

1787B - Number Factorization

First, apiiaipi is equivalent to the product of a1iai1 for pp times, so it is sufficient to set all pipi to 11.

Decomposite nn to some prime factors, greedily choose the most number of distinct prime numbers, the product is the maximum.

Solution
#include <bits/stdc++.h>
using namespace std;
#define mp make_pair
pair<int, int> s[110];
int d[110];
void get() {
	int n, l = 0, i, c;
	cin >> n;
	for (i = 2; i * i <= n; i++) {
		if (n % i == 0) {
			c = 0;
			while (n % i == 0) c++, n /= i;
			s[++l] = make_pair(c, i);
		}
	}
	if (n != 1) s[++l] = make_pair(1, n);
	sort(s + 1, s + l + 1), d[l + 1] = 1;
	for (i = l; i >= 1; i--) d[i] = d[i + 1] * s[i].second;
	int ans = 0;
	for (i = 1; i <= l; i++) if (s[i].first != s[i - 1].first) ans += d[i] * (s[i].first - s[i - 1].first);
	cout << ans << endl;
}
signed main() {
	ios::sync_with_stdio(0);
	cin.tie(0);
	int T;
	cin >> T;
	while (T--) get();
	return 0;
}

1787C - Remove the Bracket

Idea & Solution: rsj

Fact

This is the reason why the problem was named as Remove the Bracket.

Product=a1⋅a2⋅a3⋅…⋅an==a1⋅(x2+y2)⋅(x3+y3)⋅…⋅(xn−1+yn−1)⋅an==?a1⋅x2+y2⋅x3+y3⋅…⋅xn−1+yn−1⋅an.Product=a1⋅a2⋅a3⋅…⋅an==a1⋅(x2+y2)⋅(x3+y3)⋅…⋅(xn−1+yn−1)⋅an==?a1⋅x2+y2⋅x3+y3⋅…⋅xn−1+yn−1⋅an.

However, We discussed to remove it on 28th Jan in the statement. Really sorry for inconvenience of the statement!

Tutorial

1787C - Remove the Bracket

(xi−s)(yi−s)≥0(xi−s)(yi−s)≥0 tells us either min(xi,yi)≥smin(xi,yi)≥s or max(xi,yi)≤smax(xi,yi)≤s, so pickable xixi is a consecutive range.

Just consider (xi+yi)(xi+yi), remove the bracket then it turns to …+yi−1⋅xi+yi⋅xi+1+……+yi−1⋅xi+yi⋅xi+1+…. When yi−1=xi+1yi−1=xi+1, the result is constant, so we assume that yi−1<xi+1yi−1<xi+1.

If xixi does not hit the maximum, increase xixi by 11 and decrease yiyi by 11, the result will increase by yi−1yi−1 and decrease by xi+1xi+1, which means decrease by xi+1−yi−1xi+1−yi−1, always better. So xixi will either hit the maximum or the minimum. Thus, each number has only two ways of rewriting that might be optimal.

This can be done with DP.

Solution
#include <bits/stdc++.h>
using namespace std;
const int N = 200005;
long long f[N][2],x[N],y[N];
void get() {
	int i,n,s,j;
	cin>>n>>s;
	for(i=1; i<=n; i++) {
		cin>>j;
		if(i==1||i==n) x[i]=y[i]=j;
		else if(j<=s) x[i]=0,y[i]=j;
		else x[i]=s,y[i]=j-s;
	}
	f[1][0]=f[1][1]=0;
	for(i=2; i<=n; i++) {
		f[i][0]=min(f[i-1][0]+y[i-1]*x[i],f[i-1][1]+x[i-1]*x[i]);
		f[i][1]=min(f[i-1][0]+y[i-1]*y[i],f[i-1][1]+x[i-1]*y[i]);
	}
	cout<<f[n][0]<<endl;
}
int main() {
	ios::sync_with_stdio(0);
	cin.tie(0);
	int T; cin>>T;
	while(T--) get();
	return 0;
}

1787D - Game on Axis

Idea & Solution: jiangtaizhe001

Tutorial

1787D - Game on Axis

First, add directed edges from ii to i+aii+ai. If there's a path from 11 to xx satisfying x<1x<1 or x>nx>n, the game end. We consider all nodes xx satisfying x<1x<1 or x>nx>n to be the end node, and we call the path which starts at node 11 until it loops or ends the key path.

If we can end the game at first:

Let's count the opposite: the number of invalid pairs. The graph with the end node forms a tree. Changing edges not on the key path is always legal. If changing the edges on the key path, for node xx, we can only change its successor to other connected components, its precursor or itself on the tree with the end node.

For the answer, use dfs to count the number of precursors for every nodes on this tree, which is the size of subtree.

If we cannot end the game at first:

We can only change the edge on the key path, redirect them to any nodes on the tree with the end node.

Solution
#include <bits/stdc++.h>
using namespace std;
const int N = 200005;
int a[N];
int v[N]; //= 1 -> in the tree with the end node
int s[N]; //subtree size
struct E {
	int to;
	E *nex;
} *h[N];
void add(int u, int v) {
	E *cur = new E;
	cur->to = v, cur->nex = h[u], h[u] = cur;
}
void dfs(int u) {
	s[u] = v[u] = 1;
	for (E *cur = h[u]; cur; cur = cur->nex)
		dfs(cur->to), s[u] += s[cur->to];
}
void get() {
	int i, j, n;
	cin >> n;
	for (i = 1; i <= n + 1; i++)
		s[i] = v[i] = 0, h[i] = 0;
	for (i = 1; i <= n; i++) {
		cin >> a[i], a[i] = min(i + a[i], n + 1);
		if (a[i] <= 0) a[i] = n + 1;
		add(a[i], i);
	}
	dfs(n + 1); //start with the end point, dfs the tree
	long long ans = 0;
	if (v[1] == 1) {
		j = 1; do { ans -= s[j] + (n - s[n + 1] + 1), j = a[j]; }
		while (j != n + 1);
		ans += 1ll * n * (2 * n + 1);
	} else {
		j = 1; do { ans += (n + s[n + 1]), v[j] = 2, j = a[j]; }
		while (v[j] != 2);
	}
	cout << ans << endl;
}
int main() {
	ios::sync_with_stdio(0);
	cin.tie(0);
	int T;
	cin >> T;
	while (T--) get();
	return 0;
}

1787E - The Harmonization of XOR

Idea & Solution: jiangtaizhe001, rsj

Tutorial

1787E - The Harmonization of XOR

First, we observe that three subsequences can combine into one, so we only need to care about the maximum number of subsequences.

Make subsequences in the form of [a,a⊕x][a,a⊕x] as much as possible, leave [x][x] alone if possible, and the rest becomes a subsequence. This would be optimal.

Proof:

Let BB be the highest bit of xx, i.e. the BB-th bit of xx is on.

Let MM be the number of numbers from 11 to nn satisfying the BB-th bit is on.

Then the number of subsequences must be smaller than or equal to MM, since there's at least one BB-th-bit-on number in each subsequence.

These BB-th-bit-on numbers XOR xx must be smaller than themselves, so we can always obtain MM subsequences.

Bonus Problem

Bonus Problem: What if x=0x=0?

1787F - Inverse Transformation

Idea & Solution: qzhwlzy, rsj

Tutorial

1787F - Inverse Transformation

Consider another question: Given the initial permutation aa and find the permutation a′a′ on the kk-th day?

After a day, element xx will become σ(x)=axσ(x)=ax, so we can consider the numbers as nodes, and we add directed edges from xx to axax. Note that aa is a permutation, so the graph consists of several cycles. After one day, axax will become aσ(x)=aaxaσ(x)=aax – the second element after xx in the corresponding cycle. Similarly, we can conclude that axax will become the 2k2k-th element after xx.

If a cycle cc has an odd length ll. Because 2kmodl≠02kmodl≠0, the cycle will never split, the element cxcx will become c(x+2k)modlc(x+2k)modl on the kk-th day. Otherwise, if a cycle cc has an even length ll, after the first day, c1c1 will become c3c3, c3c3 will become c5c5, ……, c2l−1c2l−1 will become c1c1. And so will the even indices – that means the original cycle split into two small cycles of the same size l2l2. So, if a cycle of length l=p⋯2ql=p⋯2q will split into 2q2q small cycles of length pp after exactly qq days.

Images illustrating how a 12-node cycle split in two days.

Because the length of a cycle l≤nl≤n, so we can perform the transformation for at least lognlog⁡n days to split all the cycles of even lengths into cycles of odd lengths. Then we can use our conclusion to calculate the final permutation on the kk-th day.

Now let's back to our question. We observed that ∑i=1n1f(i)∑i=1n1f(i) means the number of cycles in the original graph. Then we need to construct the original permutation aa of the least possible cycle. As we've said before, the cycle of length l=p⋯2ql=p⋯2q will split into 2q2q small cycles of length pp after exactly qq days. So we can count all the cycles in permutation a′a′.

For cycles of odd length ll, we can combine some of them. Specifically, we can combine 2q2q cycles into a cycle of length l⋯2ql⋯2q in the original permutation aa. Note that qq must be less than or equal to kk.

And for cycles of even length ll, we can also combine them. But because they haven't been split on the kk-th day, we must combine exactly 2k2k cycles. So if the number of these cycles isn't a multiple of 2k2k, just print NO.

Solution
#include <bits/stdc++.h>
using namespace std;
const int N = 200005;
int dest[N], visit[N], ans[N], mp[N], ansinv[N], sp;
int pow2(int y, int M) {
	long long x = 2, ans = 1;
	while (y) {
		if (y & 1) ans = ans * x % M;
		x = x * x % M, y >>= 1;
	}
	return ans % M;
}
vector<int> cyc[N];
void get() {
	sp = 0;
	int n, i, j, m, z, o;
	cin >> n >> m;
	for (i = 1; i <= n; i++) cyc[i].clear();
	for (i = 1; i <= n; i++) visit[i] = 0, cin >> dest[i];
	for (i = 1; i <= n; i++) {
		if (visit[i]) continue;
		j = i, z = 0; do { ++z, visit[j] = 1, j = dest[j]; } while (j != i);
		cyc[z].push_back(i);
	}
	int lim = pow2(min(m, 20), 1e9), s = 0, d, t, num, cp;
	for (i = 1; i <= n; i++) {
		if (!cyc[i].size()) continue;
		int siz = cyc[i].size();
		if (i % 2 == 0 && siz % lim) {
			cout << "NO" << endl; return;
		} else {
			cp = -1;
			for (j = lim; j > 0; j /= 2) {
				while (siz >= j) {
					siz -= j, d = s + j * i, t = j;
					while (t--) {
						++s, ++cp, num = s, o = cyc[i][cp];
						do {
						    ans[num] = o, num += pow2(m, i * j);
						    if (num > d) num -= j * i;
						    o = dest[o];
						} while (o != cyc[i][cp]);
					}
					for (int k = sp + 1; k < sp + j * i; k++) mp[k] = k + 1;
					mp[sp + j * i] = sp + 1, sp += j * i, s = d;
				}
			}
		}
	}
	cout << "YES" << endl;
	for (i = 1; i <= n; i++) ansinv[ans[i]] = i;
	for (i = 1; i <= n; i++) cout << ans[mp[ansinv[i]]] << " \n"[i == n];
}
int main() {
	ios::sync_with_stdio(0), cin.tie(0);
	int T;
	cin >> T;
	while (T--) get();
	return 0;
}

1787G - Colorful Tree Again

Idea & Solution: rsj, 275307894a

Tutorial

1787G - Colorful Tree Again

On the original tree, all good paths are constant and one edge can only be on at most one good path.

For each color cc, find all edges of color cc, and judge if they form a simple path by counting the degree of each node on the path. If so, mark these edges and calculate the length.

When a query changes a node uu, several paths across uu are infected.

For example,

An example doodle, assume that we are destroying node 22.

If we are destroying node 22, path 8−1−2−38−1−2−3, 2−4−92−4−9, 2−52−5, and 7−6−2−107−6−2−10 will not be good paths.

A brute-force solution is to use a priority queue, delete length of bad paths, find the maximum length.

To gain better time complexity, treat two cases of influenced paths separately:

1. Only one path that goes from the ancestor of uu to its subtree, like 8−1−2−38−1−2−3.

2. All paths whose LCA is uu will be infected, like 2−4−92−4−9, 2−52−5, and 7−6−2−107−6−2−10.

This inspires us to maintain these paths according to their LCAs. That is, use priority queue on every node uu to maintain all paths whose LCA is uu.

Then use a global priority queue to maintain the answer of all paths maintained on all nodes.

UPD: An alternative implementation using segment tree comment link.

1787H - Codeforces Scoreboard

Idea & Solution: jiangtaizhe001, rsj

Something To Say

Have to declare that we built up Problem H from zero. ;)

Tutorial

1787H - Codeforces Scoreboard

Since max{bi−ki⋅t,ai}=bi−min{ki⋅t,bi−ai}max{bi−ki⋅t,ai}=bi−min{ki⋅t,bi−ai}, we can pre-calculate the sum of bibi. Let ci=bi−aici=bi−ai. Now our mission is to minimize the sum of min{ki⋅t,bi−ai}min{ki⋅t,bi−ai}.

If we assume min{ki⋅t,bi−ai}=ki⋅tmin{ki⋅t,bi−ai}=ki⋅t for some ii, sort these problems in descending order of kiki, then assign 1,2,3,…1,2,3,… to their tt in order. For min{ki⋅t,bi−ai}=bi−aimin{ki⋅t,bi−ai}=bi−ai, they can be directly calculated.

There comes a DP solution: Let fi,jfi,j be the minimum sum, when considering first ii problems (after sorting), jj problems of them satisfying min{ki⋅t,bi−ai}=ki⋅tmin{ki⋅t,bi−ai}=ki⋅t.

fi,j={fi−1,j+cimin{fi−1,j+ci,fi−1,j−1+ki⋅j}j=0j>0fi,j={fi−1,j+cij=0min{fi−1,j+ci,fi−1,j−1+ki⋅j}j>0

The time complexity is O(n2)O(n2).

We can find that fi,jfi,j is convex. Proof below:

Let gi,j=fi,j−fi,j−1gi,j=fi,j−fi,j−1.

Thus fi,j=fi,0+∑k=1jgi,kfi,j=fi,0+∑k=1jgi,k, rewrite this formula with the original recursive formula.

Then

fi,0+∑k=1jgi,k=min{fi−1,0,∑k=1jgi−1,k+ci,fi−1,0+∑k=1j−1gi−1,k+ki⋅j}fi,0+∑k=1jgi,k=min{fi−1,0,∑k=1jgi−1,k+ci,fi−1,0+∑k=1j−1gi−1,k+ki⋅j}
ci+∑k=1jgi,k=∑k=1j−1gi−1,k+min{gi−1,j+ci,ki⋅j}ci+∑k=1jgi,k=∑k=1j−1gi−1,k+min{gi−1,j+ci,ki⋅j}
Let j−1j−1 be jj
ci+∑k=1j−1gi,k=∑k=1j−2gi−1,k+min{gi−1,j−1+ci,ki⋅(j−1)}ci+∑k=1j−1gi,k=∑k=1j−2gi−1,k+min{gi−1,j−1+ci,ki⋅(j−1)}
Subtract the above two formulas from each other
gi,j=gi−1,j−1+min{gi−1,j+ci,ki⋅j}−min{gi−1,j−1+ci,ki⋅(j−1)}gi,j=gi−1,j−1+min{gi−1,j+ci,ki⋅j}−min{gi−1,j−1+ci,ki⋅(j−1)}

Because kk is monotone non-increasing, so we can use mathematical induction to proof that for jj, the increase speed of gi−1,jgi−1,j is faster than that of ki⋅jki⋅j. We can find a critical value MM that ∀j≤M,gi,j<ki⋅j∀j≤M,gi,j<ki⋅j and ∀j>M,gi,j>ki⋅j∀j>M,gi,j>ki⋅j.

So compared gigi with gi+1gi+1, the first part of the sequence remains unchanged, and the last part is added with kiki, with an additional number ki⋅jki⋅j in the middle, where jj is the maximum number satisfied gi−1,j−1≤ki⋅jgi−1,j−1≤ki⋅j.

Use the treap to maintain this sequence, which might be called the slope trick.

1787I - Treasure Hunt

Idea & Solution: 275307894a

Tutorial

1787I - Treasure Hunt

First, we observe that: maxl>porr≤p(∑i=1pai+∑i=lrai)=maxp=1n∑i=1pai+maxl,r∑i=lraimaxl>porr≤p(∑i=1pai+∑i=lrai)=maxp=1n∑i=1pai+maxl,r∑i=lrai.

Proof: We can proof it by contradiction. Define g(l,r,p)g(l,r,p) as ∑i=1pai+∑i=lrai∑i=1pai+∑i=lrai. Suppose l≤p<rl≤p<r when g(l,r,p)g(l,r,p) hits the maximum value. If ∑i=p+1rai≥0∑i=p+1rai≥0, g(l,r,r)≥g(l,r,p)g(l,r,r)≥g(l,r,p), otherwise g(l,p,p)>g(l,r,p)g(l,p,p)>g(l,r,p). So the above observation is always correct.

So we can divide the problem into two parts: maxp=1n∑i=1paimaxp=1n∑i=1pai and maxl,r∑i=lraimaxl,r∑i=lrai.

For maxp=1n∑i=1paimaxp=1n∑i=1pai, it's easy to solve it by using a stack to calculate the prefix maximum value, and then calculate the answer.

And for maxl,r∑i=lraimaxl,r∑i=lrai, consider divide and conquer. For a part [l,r][l,r],let m=⌊l+r2⌋m=⌊l+r2⌋. Define h(L,R)h(L,R) as maxL≤l,r≤R∑i=lraimaxL≤l,r≤R∑i=lrai. We want to calculate the sum of h(L,R)h(L,R) satisfied l≤L≤m<R<rl≤L≤m<R<r. Obviously h(L,R)=max(max(h(L,m),h(m+1,R)),maxL≤p≤m∑j=pmaj+maxm<p≤R∑j=m+1paj)h(L,R)=max(max(h(L,m),h(m+1,R)),maxL≤p≤m∑j=pmaj+maxm<p≤R∑j=m+1paj), and it's easy to calculate above four value in the time complexity of O(r−l)O(r−l). We can use binary search to calculate the maximum value. So the problem can be solved in the time complexity of O(nlog2n)O(nlog2⁡n).

But it's not good enough. We can solve it in the time complexity of O(nlogn)O(nlog⁡n) based on the following theorem:

Define f(p)f(p) as max1≤q≤p∑i=1qaimax1≤q≤p∑i=1qai. h(1,p)−f(p)h(1,p)−f(p) is non-decreasing.

Proof: Actually, if we extend the first theorem, we will find l>pl>p or r=pr=p :

If l>pl>p, so ∑i=p+1rai<0∑i=p+1rai<0. When we add an element at the back of the sequence: If ∑i=1lenai>∑i=1pai∑i=1lenai>∑i=1pai, pp and rr become lenlen at the same time. So Δf(len)<Δh(1,len)Δf(len)<Δh(1,len) since ∑i=p+1rai<0∑i=p+1rai<0. Otherwise if ∑i=1lenai≤∑i=1pai∑i=1lenai≤∑i=1pai, Δf(len)=0Δf(len)=0 while Δh(1,len)≥0Δh(1,len)≥0. So the theorem always holds in this circumstance.

If p=rp=r, Δf(len)=Δh(1,len)Δf(len)=Δh(1,len), so the theorem holds too.

Because of the theorem, we can replace binary search by two pointers. So the final time complexity is O(nlogn)O(nlog⁡n).

I'm really sorry for the unsatisfactory problems and the tough 3 hours ;)

However, I still hope you can enjoy the problems after the contest. The editorial hasn't been fully checked, so feel free to comment if there're errors, typos or you have questions about them. We'll check them out tomorrow. Thanks!

Tutorial of TypeDB Forces 2023 (Div. 1 + Div. 2, Rated, Prizes!)

          Time complexity? I don’t know how your iterating though all of n.(Java user here)

          • »

            mykhail0
            3 years ago, show (+1) #
            »
            »
            »
            »
            »
            »
            mykhail0
            3 years ago, hide # ^ |
            ← Rev. 3 →  
            0

            there's a fair amount of boilerplate for checking if values are out of bounds in the binsearch loop and once more after the loop. But other than that I think the code is fairly simple. WLOG let's assume x <= y. Then the outer loop iterates over x up until n (if we find solution or we know we won't find it we just break out of the loop). The inner loop binsearches on y from x to n. If y is the smallest possible (equal to x) but the formula gives an integer bigger than n, we know we are out of options so print -1 and return.

            As for TC, the solution can't iterate too high in the x, because the value of the formula grows exponentially. Inner loop (binsearch) is O(log n). So a rough upper bound is O(log^2 n)

            P.S. solve() function would look the same in Java, except for stdin/out (using cin and cout). Maybe bool -> boolean too, and you have Java code, it's all the same as C lol

+37

My funny solution for G: Basically the same as the editorial, but instead of maintaining paths by their LCA, maintain them by their highest degree node. This means that each node will affect n−−√n nodes instead of just 11, yielding an O(qn−−√lgn)O(qnlg⁡n) solution. Implemented in C++ this takes 453 ms.

→ Reply

For me, it's like:

A: acceptable.

B: prime factorization is not something that appears in d2B, and the task is quite boring.

C: Nothing wrong, but it is too hard for C I guess.

D: Easy problem with details, but nothing too wrong.

E: Actually the guesses is not quite easy to make, especially for person like me tended to prove all the guesses during the contest. So it's kind of acceptable.

F: Huge work. Very, very large work. Is these kinds of problems really suitable for codeforces? We have D and G to let participants do huge works(lol

I : Too similar to some other problems.

On E my code only make subsequences like [a,a⊕x][a,a⊕x] and overlook [x][x], but it's accepted, so does it work or it can be hacked?

→ Reply
  • »
    »
    XG0000
    3 years ago, show #
    »
    »
    XG0000
    3 years ago, hide # ^ |
    ← Rev. 5 →  
    +34

    Oh I know, if the answer is YES and x≤nx≤n, let mm be the number of subsequences [a,a⊕x][a,a⊕x], then m≥k−1m≥k−1, m≡(k−1)(mod2)m≡(k−1)(mod2).

    if m=k−1m=k−1, [x][x] will in the last subsequence, otherwise it with redundant subsequences [a,a⊕x][a,a⊕x] will in the last subsequence.

    So it's unnecessary to consider subsequence [x][x].

    0

    Let's take some correct pair a,x⊕aa,x⊕a, and suppose the numbers go into different sets AA and BB respectively. Then it's easy to see, that we can retrieve those numbers to form a pair set. So we convert AA and BB into a,x⊕aa,x⊕a and A∪B∖a,x⊕aA∪B∖a,x⊕a with both xor still equal to xx. Now the pair is in its own set and the answer isn't smaller.

The proof part of problem E:

"These BB-th-bit-on numbers XOR xx must be smaller than themselves, so we can always obtain MM subsequences."

I suppose we can not know whether the BB-th-bit-on numbers is smaller than xx since the highest digit of xx may not be the highest of these numbers.(for instance, x=2x=2, while ai=4ai=4, and x⊕aix⊕ai is 66)

→ Reply
  • »
    »
    XG0000
    3 years ago, show (+4) #
    »
    »
    XG0000
    3 years ago, hide # ^ |
     
    +3

    BB is the highest bit which xx's is on. We only consider aiai when BB-th bit of aiai is on, when x=2=(10)2x=2=(10)2, B=1B=1, ai=4=(100)2ai=4=(100)2 so the BB-th bit isn't on.

    When aiai's BB-th bit is on, ai⊕xai⊕x's BB-th bit is off, and their higher bits are equal, so we have ai⊕x<ai≤nai⊕x<ai≤n.

    → Reply

        +3

        In that case just consider the xor of all elements; the total xor value of the desired number of segments (each has an xor value of xx) is different from the give array's xor value so there is no way to construct it.

can someone explain how C can be done by DP? (I'm new to this)

→ Reply
  • »
    »
    Flatday
    3 years ago, show (+2) #
    »
    »
    Flatday
    3 years ago, hide # ^ |
    ← Rev. 3 →  
    +1

    Since we can get unique pipi and qiqi (assuming pi≤qipi≤qi ) satisfying pi+qi=aipi+qi=ai to make FF as small as possible, where xi=pi,yi=qixi=pi,yi=qi or xi=qi,yi=pixi=qi,yi=pi. Assuming we let all xi=pixi=pi and yi=qiyi=qi, we still have the possibility to swap some values of xixi and yiyi to make the value of FF is smaller.

    Taking n=5n=5 as an example, F=a1∗x2+y2∗x3+y3∗x4+y4∗a5F=a1∗x2+y2∗x3+y3∗x4+y4∗a5.

    We can let x2=p2x2=p2, so that we can determine y2=q2y2=q2, and similarly if we let x2=q2x2=q2 we can determine y2=p2y2=p2. Then we can let a1∗x2+y2∗x3a1∗x2+y2∗x3 take the minimum value if x2x2 takes the value of p2p2 or q2q2 by deciding the value of x3=x3= (p3p3 or q3q3), and at the same time the value of y3y3 is determined. Next, the value of x4x4 can be chosen as p4p4 or q4q4 so that a1∗x2+y2∗x3+y3∗x4a1∗x2+y2∗x3+y3∗x4 takes the minimum value if the value of x3x3 is p3p3 or q3q3. Finally we can use the minimum value of a1∗x2+y2∗x3+y3∗x4a1∗x2+y2∗x3+y3∗x4 and the x4x4 corresponding to its minimum value, and then y4y4 is determined, and after calculating y4∗a5y4∗a5 we get FF. in other words to find the minimum value of FF in the cases x4=p4x4=p4 and x4=q4x4=q4, and this process can be done by DP.

    It should be emphasized that we have been considering the minimum value of a part of equation FF in the case xi=pixi=pi or xi=qixi=qi, and the current worse choice may be better later, so the greedy algorithm cannot be used.

    191171920 The state in this code preserves the specific values of xixi and yiyi, which may be easier to understand compared to using 0/1.

    → Reply
    • »
      »
      »
      PrateekTewary
      3 years ago, show (+1) #
      »
      »
      »
      PrateekTewary
      3 years ago, hide # ^ |
       
      0

      In problem C, can you explain what difference it makes logically (in dp transition) if we replace the original transition,

      F[i][0] = min( F[i-1][0]+y[i-1]*x[i],F[i-1][1]+x[i-1]*x[i] ); F[i][1] = min( F[i-1][0]+y[i-1]*y[i],F[i-1][1]+x[i-1]*y[i] ); to this transition,

      F[i][0] = min( F[i-1][0]+x[i-1]*x[i],F[i-1][1]+y[i-1]*x[i] ); F[i][1] = min( F[i-1][0]+x[i-1]*y[i],F[i-1][1]+y[i-1]*y[i] ); The answer for the second transition is coming different from the original answer but both of the transitions seem correct to me logically.

      → Reply
      • »
        »
        »
        »
        Flatday
        3 years ago, show #
        »
        »
        »
        »
        Flatday
        3 years ago, hide # ^ |
         
        0

        To avoid confusion, I still use pipi and qiqi to explain. f[i][0] can be considered as choosing pipi to compute, and f[i][1] can be considered as choosing qiqi to compute. Then, as you can see from the process shown earlier, assuming that you currently choose pipi, then the next product will already have a number determined to be qiqi. So for F[i−1][0]+x[i−1]∗x[i]F[i−1][0]+x[i−1]∗x[i], F[i-1][0] has already determined one of the numbers in the next product to be y[i-1] instead of x[i-1].

        → Reply

+1

1787C - Remove the Bracket For those who as me struggle to understand tutorial, here is explanation what they want to say. They want to say that optimal selection of x, y might be only on edge cases, by way of contradiction. Suppose some is not on edge case. Then it has yi−1yi−1 and xi+1xi+1. Two cases yi−1<xi+1yi−1<xi+1 and yi−1>xi+1yi−1>xi+1 lead to contradiction based on things from the editorial (look into it). And in the remaining case yi−1=xi+1yi−1=xi+1 we can pick edge x, y without any loss.

→ Reply

In problem C, can anyone explain what difference it makes logically (in dp transition) if we replace the original transition,

F[i][0] = min( F[i-1][0]+y[i-1]*x[i],F[i-1][1]+x[i-1]*x[i] );
F[i][1] = min( F[i-1][0]+y[i-1]*y[i],F[i-1][1]+x[i-1]*y[i] );

to this transition,

F[i][0] = min( F[i-1][0]+x[i-1]*x[i],F[i-1][1]+y[i-1]*x[i] );
F[i][1] = min( F[i-1][0]+x[i-1]*y[i],F[i-1][1]+y[i-1]*y[i] );

The answer for the second transition is coming different from the original answer but both of the transition seem correct to me logically.

→ Reply
  • »
    »
    XG0000
    3 years ago, show #
    »
    »
    XG0000
    3 years ago, hide # ^ |
     
    0

    They are just different. For each ii we have xixi and yiyi, one of them multiply by last number and another by next one.

    As an example, for fi−1,0fi−1,0 we used xi−1xi−1, then yi−1yi−1 is left, so for fi,0fi,0 we should use yi−1yi−1 and xixi then the formula is fi,0=fi−1,0+yi−1∗xifi,0=fi−1,0+yi−1∗xi.

    → Reply

Olympia
3 years ago, show #

Olympia
3 years ago, hide # |
 
+77

I have a different solution for problem GG.

The idea is to run a BFS and label the edges according to when they were processed by the BFS. Below, I have the tree, with the edge colors and labels.

Now, we can turn these edge labels into color labels. Define the label of a color to the minimum label of one of its edges. For instance, the label of the red path is 1, and the label of the yellow path is 18. The label of the purple color is 13.

Now, whenever we perform an update (blocking or unblocking a node), we are updating at most two continuous range of labels. Say, for instance, we update the root of the tree. Then, we're blocking "red" and "blue" (label 1 and 2). If we were to update the node from which edges 4,5,6 protrude, then we're blocking "red" and "blue (4 and 5).

Now, updating continuous ranges of intervals can be done with a segment tree. Let the index of the segment tree be the label of each color. So segmentTree[label[i]] = sum of weights of path with color i.

Each time, we block some "path", we can subtract a big number from that contiguous range of the segment tree. Each time we unblock that path add back that big number. Now, it's just range minima of whole segment tree and range add.

→ Reply
»

E: "the rest becomes a subsequence".

Shouldn't it be "adding the rest to one of the other subsequences"? If there is a solution then the rest must xor to zero (obviously the rest cannot xor to xx) so we can add the rest to any other subsequence.

+14

Here am I with my toxic grumblings about the quality of editorials again. Nothing new, I just decided to try reading editorials again and once again I ask myself why the editorial authors hate their readers so much.

I'm just opening the solution for D and the first thing I see is

int a[N], v[N], s[N];
struct E {
	int to;
	E *nex;
} *h[N];

The heck is a? the heck is v? the heck is s? Why the list of edges is h?
Probably it should be relatively easy for the typical audience of this problem to deduce that E means edge even if you don't care about the others, but a/v/s/h is just too much. I won't even ask why you needed a linked list.

As a result, decoding and deobfuscating the heck is written in the "author's solution" easily competes by difficulty with the original problem.

I understand that codeforces doesn't enforce the quality of editorials and it is done by putting the minimum effort possible, but put at least the minimum effort into making it readable, this is so below any bar.

I find it hard to comprehend that people spend some supposedly huge amount of time on coming up with and polishing those problems, but cannot find some minutes to make the code they just submitted at least a bit readable before attaching to the editorial

→ Reply
